SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by extensive experiments: 21 automated metrics (WBMs and GBMs) were evaluated across three datasets and three systems, with human judgements collected (3 raters per output) and ICC reported (overall ICC = 0.45). Appropriate statistical analyses are used (Spearman correlations, Wilcoxon signed-rank test, Williams’ test, p < 0.05) and known caveats are acknowledged (e.g. Stanford parser score not designed for grammaticality). Minor concerns remain about reliance on moderate inter-annotator agreement and limited detail on construction/ablation of the proposed RAINBOW metric in the provided excerpt, but these do not undermine the main experimental conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper makes several novel contributions: it is (claimed to be) the first to consider grammar-based metrics (GBMs) for sentence-level NLG and to target end-to-end data-driven NLG comparing three approaches, and it proposes a new combined metric, RAINBOW. These extend prior evaluation work (e.g. Reiter & Belz, Kilickaya et al.) rather than rehashing them, so the contribution is a notable and non-trivial advance, though it builds on an active existing line of metric-evaluation research.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-structured (introduction, datasets, metrics, human data collection, analyses, related work, conclusion) and lists its contributions clearly early on. Extensive tables and appendices (e.g. Tables 7–14) present detailed results and the binning/error analyses are explained. The provided excerpt contains some duplicated/fragmented text, but the overall presentation and use of examples (MR/reference) and summary statistics are clear.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental scope is comprehensive: 21 metrics, three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS), and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), plus detailed error analyses (e.g. binning by rating, MR-type analysis) and discussion of dataset- and system-specific behavior. The authors discuss limitations (parser-based grammaticality proxy, moderate ICC) and make code/data available. One gap in the provided text is a lack of explicit ablation or component-wise analysis of the RAINBOW metric in the excerpt, which would strengthen reproducibility and understanding.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and widely-practiced problem (reliance on automatic evaluation metrics in NLG) and provides evidence that commonly used metrics weakly reflect human judgements while proposing a stronger composite metric (RAINBOW, reported up to ρ = 0.81). These findings and public release of code/data are likely to influence evaluation practices in NLG, though the long-term transformative effect will depend on adoption and further validation of RAINBOW across more domains.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (evaluation of NLG metrics and proposal of a new metric) is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The paper uses dataset- and system-level experiments, standard baselines (BLEU, METEOR, CIDEr, etc.), and accepted statistical analyses, and it positions itself relative to prior work, making it highly appropriate for such conferences.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the proposed approach against a broad set of appropriate baselines (many WBMs and novel GBMs) on the same data and evaluation conditions, reports statistical significance, and discusses how results vary by dataset/system and prior contradictory findings. The excerpt does not show an exhaustive ablation of RAINBOW components or comparisons to every recent learned evaluation metric, so some additional baseline/ablation comparisons would further strengthen the claims.
